title: [PDF] Commercial Bank Examination Manual, 3000—CAPITAL, EARNINGS, LIQUIDITY, AND SENSITIVITY TO MARKET RISK
summary: This source is a section of the Federal Reserve's Commercial Bank Examination Manual (Section 3000), dated May 2021 with an effective date of November 2020, detailing the supervisory assessment framework for evaluating a state member bank's Capital, Earnings, Liquidity, and Sensitivity to market risk (CELS). The document describes Regulation Q requirements, capital components (CET1, Additional Tier 1, Tier 2), and risk-weighted asset calculations under Basel standards.
key_points: Regulation Q (12 CFR Part 217) was adopted in 2013 by the Fed, FDIC, and OCC to replace general risk-based capital requirements and address weaknesses highlighted during the 2008-09 financial crisis
The three components of regulatory capital are: (1) Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1), (2) Additional Tier 1, and (3) Tier 2 capital
Common Equity Tier 1 capital is the highest-quality and most loss-absorbing form of capital, primarily composed of common stock and retained earnings
Risk-based capital ratio is calculated by dividing qualifying capital (numerator) by risk-weighted assets (denominator)
Capital regulation is important because deposit insurance provides banks incentive to increase leverage beyond what the market would permit
The document covers Prompt Corrective Action (PCA) provisions under section 38 of the Federal Deposit Insurance Act
why_it_matters: This document is a bank supervisory/regulatory reference manual, not a market-moving source. It describes how Federal Reserve examiners assess bank capital adequacy under Regulation Q and Basel standards. It has no relevance to macro catalysts or market-moving events.
important_numbers: $3 billion (threshold for Small BHC policy statement applicability)
